coredata.dc:title: Yellow emissive carbon dots in ludox silica matrix with anticancer activity for enhanced imaging of developed sweat latent fingermarks
coredata.dc:creator: Kainth, S.
Sharma, V.
Bhagat, M.
Basu, S.
coredata.dc:description: The recognition of fingermarks plays a vital role during a criminal investigation. In the current scenario, fluorescent nano-powders emerged as a potential candidate for latent fingermark detection. We pioneer the use of microwave technology for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) with the cationic surfactant (cetylpyridinium chloride). The surfactant-derived CDs exhibit yellow fluorescence and emission at 545 nm with high quantum yield of (QY∼34%). The prepared CDs also demonstrated amphiphilic properties. These luminous CDs were combined with ludox HS-30 to develop fluorescent hybrid nano-powders for the detection of sweat latent fingermarks on a non-porous surface. The ludox@CDs accumulate particle size 11.36 ± 1.235 nm and surface area 111 cm3/g−1. The fluorescent hybrid nano-powders emerged to be successful in creating high contrast and accurate latent fingermark images. Furthermore, cytotoxicity studies on breast cell lines (fR-2 and MCF-7) were conducted by using ludox@CDs. The results revealed that ludox@CDs were less toxic and biocompatible in comparison to the commercialized dusting reagents. Fortunately, ludox@CDs demonstrated anticancer efficacy against MCF-7 breast cancer cell lines. Thus, our findings herald a new age for fluorescent hybrid nano-powders in criminal investigation and biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ludox HS-30
Cytotoxicity
Fingermark detection
Antitumor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of the latent fingermark using ludox@CDs
Optical properties of CDs and ludox@CDs
Evaluation of cytotoxicity using MTT assay
DAPI staining for apoptosis determination in MCF-7 cells
Colony formation assay in MCF-7 cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images for average particle size of ludox@CDs at different magnifications. CDs, carbon dots; HRTEM, high-resolution transmission electron microscope.
FTIR spectra of a) CDs, ludox HS-30 in the presence and absence of CDs and b) inset of ludox@CDs spectra. CDs, carbon dots; FTIR, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy.
Surface composition of ludox@CDs: a) EDS, b) XPS survey for free CDs and ludox@CDs, c) high-resolution XPS spectra and fitting results for C 1s and d) Si 2p. XPS, X-ray photoelectron microscopy.

(a) Nitrogen adsorption/desorption analysis for ludox HS-30 in the absence and presence of CDs and (b) BJH pore size distribution for ludox HS-30 and ludox@CDs. BJH, Barrett Joyner Halenta; CDs, carbon dots.
Photoluminescence spectra of ludox HS-30 at a) different concentration of CDs and b) chromaticity diagram for ludox@CDs. CDs, carbon dots.
Photographs of fingermarks stained on glass slide using a) ludox–HS–30, b) ludox@CDs and c) magnified picture of ludox@CDs with minutiae labeled illuminated. CDs, carbon dots.
DAPI staining for apoptosis determination in MCF-7 cells using a) A1 = ludox@CDs, b) A = ludox (3%), c) B = ludox (1.5%) and d) C= ludox (0.5%). CDs, carbon dots.
Design of synthetic protocol to obtain ludox@CDs nano-powder. CDs, carbon dots.
% Growth inhibition of test samples at 24 h and 48 h in MCF-7 and fR2 cells as determined by MTT assay.
IC50 values of test samples at 24 h and 48 h were determined by MTT assay.

head.ce:title: Yellow emissive carbon dots in ludox silica matrix with anticancer activity for enhanced imaging of developed sweat latent fingermarks

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Affiliate Faculty—TIET-Virginia Tech Center of Excellence in Emerging Materials, Thapar Institute of Engineering and Technology, Patiala 147004, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Affiliate Faculty—TIET-Virginia Tech Center of Excellence in Emerging Materials
Thapar Institute of Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Affiliate Faculty-TIET-Virginia Tech Center of Excellence in Emerging Materials, Thapar Institute of Engineering and Technology, Patiala 147004, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Forensic Science & Criminology, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Forensic Science & Criminology, Panjab University, Chandigarh-160014, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biotechnology, University of Jammu, Jammu 180006-180006, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biotechnology, University of Jammu, Jammu-180006-180006, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The recognition of fingermarks plays a vital role during a criminal investigation. In the current scenario, fluorescent nano-powders emerged as a potential candidate for latent fingermark detection. We pioneer the use of microwave technology for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) with the cationic surfactant (cetylpyridinium chloride). The surfactant-derived CDs exhibit yellow fluorescence and emission at 545 nm with high quantum yield of (QY∼34%). The prepared CDs also demonstrated amphiphilic properties. These luminous CDs were combined with ludox HS-30 to develop fluorescent hybrid nano-powders for the detection of sweat latent fingermarks on a non-porous surface. The ludox@CDs accumulate particle size 11.36 ± 1.235 nm and surface area 111 cm3/g−1. The fluorescent hybrid nano-powders emerged to be successful in creating high contrast and accurate latent fingermark images. Furthermore, cytotoxicity studies on breast cell lines (fR-2 and MCF-7) were conducted by using ludox@CDs. The results revealed that ludox@CDs were less toxic and biocompatible in comparison to the commercialized dusting reagents. Fortunately, ludox@CDs demonstrated anticancer efficacy against MCF-7 breast cancer cell lines. Thus, our findings herald a new age for fluorescent hybrid nano-powders in criminal investigation and biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: In a criminal investigation, the uniqueness of fingermarks is the effective mark in forensic sciences [1–4]. These fingermarks are categorized in three forms at crime scenes: visible, indented, and latent fingermarks (LFPs) [5]. During crime investigation, the main focus is given to LFPs due to invisibility through naked eyes and requires different detection methods to achieve high-resolution visualization. A variety of detection methods are proposed depending on residues (sweat and blood) present on the human fingers [1,5,6]. These methods involve spectroscopy, powder dusting, and chemical staining [7,8]. Among these methods, chemical staining is commonly used by technicians for the detection of blood-based fingermarks due to the interaction of hemoglobin with chemical reagents. There are several commercialized reagents used for chemical staining such as ninhydrin,1,8-diazafluoren-9-one and cyanoacrylate for resolute images [9]. However, the surface properties and morphology of the chemical reagents restrict the utilization of the chemical staining method for sweat-based fingermarks detection. In addition, the reagents need a long time and heating to induce reaction with LFPs for their proper development. Consequently, the formation of fingermarks from sweat residue remains a key problem in criminal investigations [10].

Moreover, to identify the extraction of DNA from fingermark residues, the toxicity of a staining reagent is a major concern. To address the issue of detecting fingermark in sweat residues, the fluorescent nanoparticles appeared as a potential source [11–13][A-C]. The fluorescent nanoparticles possess low toxicity and emit strong fluorescence by exciting at specific light. This leads to high-resolution detection of LFPs [14]. Among several traditional fluorescent nanomaterials in the forensic world, quantum dots (QDs) emerged as a promising chemical reagent for the detection of LFPs [15–17]. However, utilization of water-soluble QDs resulted in weak and opaque images of LFPs. In addition, metal-doped QDs are also developed to improve the contrast of the images [18]. The synthesis protocol and utilization of heavy metals to develop metal-doped QDs are complex and limit their practical usage.

Predominantly, carbon dots (CDs) have exclusive merits over QDs such as low cost, plasticity in surface chemistry, and biological properties. These unique properties have made CDs a potential fluorescent material for LPFs detection. In 2018, fingermark development was done by immersing the lipophilic white fluorescent CDs dissolved in organic solvents. The researchers used the spray method to develop fingermarks using red fluorescent CDs dissolved in 0.1 M hydrochloric acid [19,20]. Blue emissive CDs were also implemented for fingermark development using spray and powder methods. However, these CDs are not suitable for commercialization due to opacity in fingermark images and interference with developing surfaces. Therefore, well-designed fluorescent CDs are required that could raise fingermark with high firmness and contrast. To achieve this goal, three major steps must be considered before developing fingermark chemical reagents using CDs. Firstly, CDs must be fabricated by using a significant precursor to interact with LFP residues. Secondly, an appropriate dispersion medium with optimum pH is crucial to avoid corrosion of surfaces exhibiting fingermarks such as plastic and leather. Finally, the emission color of CDs must exist in the longrange (500–600 nm) to enhance the integrity of fingermarks and avoid strong background interference. These steps lead to the development of CDs with bright fluorescence in solution and are suitable for LFPs detection through the spray method. However, the solution casting is not suitable for permeable substrates such as cloth, paper, and wood [15,21]. Therefore, it necessitates the development of solid-state CDs for LFPs detection through the powder method. Solid-state CDs can be prepared using the hot air–drying method. This method suppresses the fluorescence of CDs due to the aggregation effect. Thus, CDs hybrid powders were produced to reduce the aggregation effect. While their implementation proved to be ineffective for fingermark identification due to low fluorescence [22,23]. Currently, a few teams are involved to investigate the effect of various template sources such as starch, polymer, and silica powder to control the CDs' fluorescence. This approach can preserve the interparticle spacing between adjacent CDs particles during the drying process [24]. In addition, CDs are a fully biocompatible material and suitable for biomedical applications such as in vitro imagining of transfection and cytotoxicity due to ultra-small size for less than 10 nm. Till date, silica-based solid-state CDs are not examined for anticancer activity. Moreover, there are no reports on LFPs detection using yellow emissive ludox HS-30@CDs hybrid powder.

In this work, we have developed eco-friendly and cost-effective fluorescent nano-powders for the detection of sweat-based fingermarks. The synthesis of surfactant enriched colloidal CDs was done by using a microwave synthesizer. Afterward, CDs nano-powder were obtained by the hot drying method using ludox HS-30 template. The prepared CDs nano-powder were utilized for the development and detection of LFPs on the non-porous surface through the dusting approach. In vitro cytotoxic and antiproliferative response was also evaluated on normal and cancerous breast cell lines. This study serves as a base for the development of next-generation fluorescent carbon-based nano-powders for biomedical and forensic applications.

Ludox HS-30 and cetylpyridinium chloride were purchased from Loba Chemie Pvt Ltd (spectrograde), India. All these reagents were used directly by preparing their aqueous solution using distilled water (18.2 MΩ.cm, Millipore). The CDs were synthesized using Multiwave-300 microwave synthesis reactor (Anton Paar, USA). LS-55 spectro-fluorophotometer (Perkin Elmer) was used to study its optoelectronic properties. A hot air oven was used for the preparing powder CDs on ludox HS-30 matrix The synthesized ludox@CDs nano-powder were characterized by a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM), (TALOS F200S G2, 200 KV, FEG, CMOS Camera 4K × 4K, In Column EDS detectors) for the analysis of its surface morphology and particle size using a drop-casting technique. Moreover, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron microscopy (XPS) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy techniques were carried out to know the better insight on the ludox@CDs surface.

Microwave technique was followed to obtain CDs from cetylpyridinium chloride (CPC) under basic conditions. At first, CPC (12 mg) was dissolved in NaOH solution (2 ml, 10−2 M). The mixture was kept at 130 °C for 2 min under microwave heating after which transparent color turns into yellow colloidal solutions. This colloidal solution was purified using centrifugation to separate heavy particles, and the supernatant was further passed from syringe filters to have pure CDs.

The PLQY of all four NCDs was evaluated using quinine sulphate (φr = 0.546) [25] as a reference solution dissolved in 0.01 M H2SO4 by subsequent equation (1):
(1)
φ =
φ r
×
(
I /
I r
)
×
(
A r
/ A
)
×
(
η 2
/
η 2
r
)
where ‘φ’ and ‘φr’ denotes PLQY of the sample and reference, respectively, ‘I’ is the integrated area of fluorescence intensity, ‘A’ is the absorbance value, and ‘η’ is refractive index.

Synthesis of solid-state CDs was done by mixing an appropriate amount of ludox HS-30 (3%) with a colloidal solution of CDs (0.5 ml) as shown in Scheme 1 . Afterward, the mixture was converted into solid state by keeping it for 6 h in a hot air oven. Last of all, emission color of powder was logged by exciting at 365 nm using a UV lamp. This prepared nano-powder was utilized for fingermark detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of the latent fingermark using ludox@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The powder dusting method was followed for the development of fingermarks on glass slides. Primarily, before depositing the hands of the latent print of the donor were washed and allow for normal secretion of sweat. Then prints were deposited on a glass surface which was further developed using synthesized powder and validated.

Cytotoxicity of ludox@CDs was analyzed by MTT assay using the cancer cell line (MCF-7, breast) and normal cell line (fR-2, breast). The MTT assay is a standard colorimetric assay for determining the viability of cells. The cells were seeded at 5000 cells/well in a 96 well-plate and were treated with different concentrations of test samples for a while of either 24 h or 48 h in separate plates at 37 °C in a humidified incubator (5% CO2). Four hours before the completion of treatment time, the culture medium was removed and 20 μl/well MTT solution was added. The MTT concentration was kept at 2.5 mg/ml of the solution, and plates were incubated for 4 h. After incubation, MTT solution was removed and DMSO was added (100μl/well) to the microtiter plate and further shaken at room temperature for 1 h. A purple-colored complex was observed, and color darkened over the 1 h incubation period. After the 1 h incubation, formazan precipitate was dissolved by pipetting each well until the precipitate vanished. Absorbance was taken at 570 nm using a microplate reader. The mean OD570 of the percentage of controls was assumed 100% viability. The IC50 values were calculated using GraphPad Prism software (Version 5.0) [26].

MCF-7 cells, after trypsinization, were seeded in 4 well chamber slides at a density of 35 × 103 cells/per well and treated with various concentrations of test samples. The cells were incubated for 24 h and washed twice with chilled PBS and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 20 min. Furthermore, 1% Triton X-100 was used to permeabilize the cells followed by incubation of 15min at room temperature in dark with 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) containing mounting medium. Post mounting, the slides were sealed and stored at 4○C. Furthermore, the images were captured at a magnification of 20 X [27].

MCF-7 cells were plated at a seeding density of (1 × 103 cells/well) in six-well tissue culture grade plates for 24 h. The culture medium was replaced by a fresh medium after 24 h, and cells were treated with various concentrations of test samples/vehicle for 5 days at 37 °C and incubated in 5% CO2. Later, the obtained colonies were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde and stained with 0.5% crystal violet solution. The colonies from the plates were counted and averaged from the observed fields randomly (n = 3) and photographed.

The morphology and size of CDs and ludox@CDs nano-powder were determined from HRTEM analysis as shown in Fig. 1 . The results confirm the spherical uniformity in ludox@CDs nano-powder with an average particle size of 11.36 ± 1.235 nm. Moreover, the ultra-small particles with an average particle size of 3.59 ± 0.02 nm were also perceived (Fig. 1a and b) which confirmed the embedment of CDs on the ludox HS-30 surface. The integration of CDs on the ludox HS-30 surface was evaluated through FTIR analysis as shown in Fig. 2 . The spectrum recorded for ludox HS-30 revealed three characteristic peaks at 1151, 806, and 468 cm−1 which correspond to Si–O–Si asymmetric, symmetric stretching, and bending vibration, respectively [28–31]. Another two peaks have appeared in the range of 1800–2000 cm−1 which correspond to the overtones of Si–O–Si stretching vibrations. Furthermore, broadband at 3471 cm−1 was spotted in ludox HS-30, which was attributed to absorbed water and H-bonded silanol (OH) groups, as well as a strong peak at 1640 cm−1 owing to Si–OH deformation vibration [32,33]. The FTIR spectrum of CDs were also recorded, and it displayed broad peak at 3313 cm−1 which attributes to the O–H/N–H stretching. Another spectral band was also observed at 1639 cm−1 which attributes to C=N stretching. However, the addition of CDs on the ludox HS-30 surface cause change in the amplitude of the spectral bands corresponds to Si–O–H and Si–O–Si stretching. Subsequently, additional peaks at 2845, 2933, and 1489 cm−1 were appeared due to the stretching vibration of the –CH3, –CH2 groups and the development of the Si–C bond between CDs and ludox HS-30 (Fig. 2a and b) [33]. Thus, this surface analysis suggests that CDs have strong chemical and physical interaction with oxygen segments of ludox HS-30 which contributed to maintain the matrix structural integrity [24,32,33].

The percentile elemental composition of ludox@CDs was evaluated from energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDS) as shown in Fig. 3 a. The analysis suggests that this nano-powder accumulates different elements such as C (26.5%), O (58.16%), Si (15.17%), and N (0.17%) respectively. Furthermore, an XPS survey was done for ludox@CDs and free CDs (Fig. 3b). The XPS findings displayed four distinctive peaks at 101.8, 151.1, 282.5, and 530.6 eV in ludox@CDs owing to Si 2p, Si 2s, C 1s, and O 1s, respectively. However, peaks at 101.8 and 151.1 eV owing to Si 2p and Si 2s were absent in free CDs. These results propose that CDs were effectively integrated into the ludox HS-30 template. The XPS spectra for the C 1s and Si 2p were deconvoluted to validate the chemical interaction of CDs with ludox HS-30. The C 1s spectrum was deconvoluted into two peaks at 284.05 (C=C) and 282.45 eV (Si–C) [32] as shown in Fig. 3c. Si 2p deconvoluted spectrum involves two peaks at 101.2 and 102.7 eV corresponding to Si–C and Si–O/Si–O–C bonds (Fig. 3d). These findings indicate that embedment of CDs occurred within the ludox HS-30 via chemical interactions [34].

The effect of CDs impregnation on the pore size, shapes, and porosity of the silica template was further investigated using nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms. The studies proposed that ludox HS-30 powder exhibited a hysteresis loop with an H2 shape which occurs due to the presence of mesopores with small width and cylindrical pore shape in the narrow range of pore necks, whereas hysteresis loop of CDs embedded ludox HS-30 exhibiting H1 type physisorption isotherm i.e. the material has a narrow range of uniform mesopores (Fig. 4 a). It suggests that pores had been created through preblocking/percolation of CDs [35]. In addition, the Barrett Joyner Halenta (BJH) method was used to measure the pore width and pore size distribution of all samples. It was found that the average pore size changes from 2 nm for ludox HS-30 to 10 nm for ludox@CDs as shown in Fig. 4b. This increment in the pore size occurred due to shrinkage forces and to maintain the structural integrity during the drying process. The total surface area was also calculated using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller analysis of nitrogen adsorption. The results revealed that the incorporation of CDs into ludox HS-30 causes a decline in the surface area of ludox@CDs (122–111 m2g-1) due to internal pore strain.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CDs and ludox@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, the cationic surfactant (CPC) act as fluorescence quenchers while its conversion into CDs accumulates good PL properties. The fabrication of CDs from cationic surfactants mainly depends on the pH of the reaction medium. If the pH of surfactant solution exists, at lower value, then no emission band appeared at visible region [36]. Although to initiate the conversion of non-fluorescent CPC into CDs the pH of the reaction medium needs to be adjusted in the range of 11–12. To sustain the basic pH of reaction medium the concentration of NaOH was taken into consideration. Thus, the PL experiment were performed to explain the role of NaOH on the synthesis of CDs from CPC. The PL spectra of reaction product were recorded at different concentration of NaOH (0–0.1 M) as mentioned in Supporting Information Fig. S1a. The studies revealed that the pH of reaction medium need to be setup at 0.01 M concentration of NaOH. At this concentration of NaOH the carbonization and deprotonation of CPC occurs which assist in the conversion of the cationic surfactant into CDs. If the concentration of NaOH exceeded from 0.01 M, it accelerates the carbonization of CPC which leads to formation of black precipitates with weak fluorescence. Thus, optimized concentration of NaOH is required to maintain the pH of the surfactant solution. Furthermore, the impact of different concentration of CPC (3.53–6 mM) on PL response of CDs were recorded (Fig S1b). The studies shown that at lower range of CPC, the emission of CDs exists in visible region at 545 nm when excited at 380 nm. While the rise in the concentration of CPC suppresses the PL intensity of CDs with no shift in the emission peak. In recent reports, CDs were synthesized from CPC (0.76 g, 42.5 mM) and NaOH (1 M) which comprise a shoulder band at 475 and a broad band at 545 nm when excited at 420 nm with QY∼1.2%. Therefore, to improve the fluorescence response of CDs, the method was modified by optimizing the amount of CPC (12 mg, 3.53 mM) and NaOH (10−2 M) to produced yellow emissive CDs with emission at 545 nm on excitation at 380 nm and QY∼34%. These CDs also accumulates positive surface charge density of +63 mV because of the presence of CPC on its surface that induces amphiphilic characteristics and stability in CDs [36].

The as-prepared CDs were further mixed with ludox HS-30 for the fabrication of fluorescent nano-powder. Firstly, PL spectra of ludox HS-30 were recorded at different concentration as shown in Fig. S2a. The recorded PL spectra showed that ludox HS-30 accumulates emission band at 475 nm while its emission intensity get enhanced with the rise in its concentration (3%–30%). Consequently, the PL spectra of CDs were recorded at different concentration of ludox HS-30 as shown in Fig. S2b. The studies revealed that at high concentration, ludox HS-30 suppress the emission of CDs and exhibited emission at 475 nm of its own. So, to eliminate the emission hindrance by ludox HS-30 the minimum concentration i.e. 3% was chosen. Furthermore, different concentration of CDs (4.7–47 mg) was integrated into the ludox HS-30 template, and its PL spectra were recorded (as shown in Fig. 5 a). The spectra showed sharp rise in the emission intensity at 545 nm with the rise in the concertation of CDs in the presence of ludox HS-30 (3%). However, on hot air treatment of the prepared mixture at higher concentration of CDs and ludox HS-30 (3%), the charring of material occurs because of the overlapping in the molecular orbital of nearby CDs. Thus, moderate concentration of CDs (21 mg, 3.53 mM) was integrated into ludox HS-30 (3%) matrix to obtain fluorescent nano-powder and to use as them for latent fingermark development and imaging.

The emission color of the nano-powder was evaluated from Commission Internationale de l' Ecairage (CIE) coordinates. The CIE coordinates calculated for ludox HS-30 (3%) at 380 nm excitation wavelength and are 0.19, and 0.24 which correspond to the shade of near blue emission (as shown in Fig. 5b). However, on the addition of CDs (4.7 mg) into the ludox HS-30, the coordinates shifted to 0.23 and 0.30 i.e. shifted towards near to white emission. Furthermore, increasing the concentration of CDs up to 47 mg, the color coordinates shift from white to green and then yellow emission with color coordinates 0.32 and 0.44 at 47 mg concentration, respectively. Fig. 5b clearly depicts this tuning of emission wavelength/color on increasing the concentration of CDs into the ludox HS-30. The CIE 1931 Chromaticity color coordinates along with its color rendering index and correlated color temperature (K) values for ludox HS-30 (3%) to ludox@CDs with the concentration of CDs 4.7–47 mg are shown in Table ST1.

The powder dusting method was followed for the development of latent fingermarks on flat surfaces like glass slides. Before depositing the fingermarks to the various surfaces, the volunteer donors cleansed their hands with soap and then dried them in the air. The fingermarks were applied by rubbing the tips of the fingers and thumbs together smoothly and allowed them for normal secretion of sweat. Then ludox@CDs powder was gently brushed on the latent fingermarks for its detection. The extra powder particles in the form of lumps sometimes adhere to the ridges if any are blown off. Finally, under 365 nm UV light, the fingermarks were visualized and then captured with a digital camera (Fig. 6 a and b) shows the clear development of latent fingermarks using ludox HS-30 and ludox@CDs on the glass slide surfaces. It can be seen that the minutiae details such as core, delta, ridge ending, bifurcation, enclosure, ridge dots and so on are visible in the developed fingermark as shown in Fig. 6c. It reveals the potential of ludox@CDs nano-powder as a fingermark sensor on non-porous surfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of cytotoxicity using MTT assay
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the cytotoxic potential of CDs, an MTT assay was performed in breast cancer cell line MCF-7 and normal breast epithelial cell line fR-2. The test was carried out at different concentrations of each test sample (0.002, 0.02, 0.2, and 2.0 mg/ml) and at different time points (24 h and 48 h). The percent growth inhibition of MCF-7 and fR-2 cells at 24 h and 48 h for ludox@CDs and ludox HS-30 at a different concentration from 3% to 0.5% i.e. abbreviated as A1 A, B, and C were mentioned in Table 1 . The cell viability for A1, A, B, and C at 24 h and 48 h, respectively, were also evaluated against MCF-7 and fR-2 cell lines (Fig. S3 and S4). The results revealed that A1 (2 mg/ml) causes growth inhibition (GI) of 48% after 24 h and 72% after 48 h in comparison to vehicle-treated cells in MCF-7 cells. Similarly, in the fR-2 cell line, A1 showed 18% GI after 24 h and 33% GI after 48 h. In MCF-7 cells, sample A (2 mg/ml) showed 52% GI after 24 h and 100% GI after 48 h, indicating potent cytotoxicity at this dose. Of note, sample A (2 mg/ml) caused 32% GI after 24 h and 53% GI after 48 h in fR-2 cells. Sample B (2 mg/ml) is cytotoxic to breast cancer cell line with 29% GI after 24 h and 59% GI after 48 h in comparison to a normal cell line which showed 16% GI after 24 h and 41% after 48 h. Sample C, at 2 mg/ml is cytotoxic to MCF-7 cells with 16% GI after 24 h and 42% GI after 48 h. Similarly, in the fR-2 cell line, sample C showed 8% GI after 24 h and 22% GI after 48 h. These investigations found that the integration of CDs into Ludox HS-30 (silica template) decreased the cytotoxicity against normal breast epithelial cell line fR-2 and increased its anticancer potency.

For a better understanding of the cytotoxic potential of the tested compounds, we calculated the IC50 values of the respective compounds against MCF-7 and fR-2 cells at 24 h and 48 h from the percent growth inhibition data (Table 2 ). IC50 is a better determinant compared to percent growth inhibition and percent cell viability as it represents the half-maximal inhibitory concentration and is a measure of the potency of a substance in inhibiting cell viability. Our results revealed that at 24 h, excluding A, the rest of the compounds (A1, B, and C) had IC50 > 2 mg/ml for both the cell lines tested. At 24 h the IC50 for A was 1.5 mg/ml. Interestingly at 48 h, the IC50 values observed against breast cancer cells (MCF-7) for A1, A, and B were 0.424, 0.115 and 0.448 mg/ml, respectively. Compound C had IC50 > 2 mg/ml against MCF-7 cells at 48 h. However, in normal cell line fR-2 at 48 h, apart from A1 (IC50 of 1.7 mg/ml), the rest of the compounds had IC50 > 2 mg/ml. Upon diligent analysis of the cytotoxicity results, we concluded that at 24 h the compounds are non-toxic up to a concentration of 1 mg/ml against the cancerous MCF-7 cells. However, at the 48-h time-point, the compounds show cytotoxicity against MCF-7 cells. Interestingly, the tested compounds were found to be less toxic against normal breast cells (fR-2) in comparison to MCF-7 cells. This might indicate selective targeting of cancer cells over normal cells. In conclusion, cytotoxicity potential of sample A> sample A1 > sample B > sample C with more propensity against cancer cells than normal cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DAPI staining for apoptosis determination in MCF-7 cells
ce:section.ce:para: Apoptosis or programmed cell death is a single-cell phenomenon that is characterized by nuclear condensation, nuclear fragmentation and the formation of apoptotic bodies. To determine the apoptosis-inducing potential of these test compounds, we employed the DAPI staining assay in MCF-7 cells. DAPI is a fluorescent stain that specifically binds to AT regions in DNA. Cells treated with A1 and A (100 μg/ml) showed distinctive induction of apoptosis in a subset of cells as is evident from their nuclear condensation and fragmentation indicated by red arrows (Fig. 7 ). In contrast, cells treated with B and C revealed no alterations in the nuclear morphology. Similarly, nuclei of vehicle-treated MCF-7 cells showed characteristics of normal nuclei with well-defined and intact morphology. These findings corroborate our MTT results and unveil that compound A, in particular, and to a lesser extent compound A1 can be cytotoxic to cancer cells by inducing apoptosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colony formation assay in MCF-7 cells
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of test samples on the proliferation of MCF-7 cells was investigated by colony formation assay (Fig. S5). This assay is an in vitro cell survival assay based on the ability of a single cell to grow in a colony and is routinely performed to determine the clonogenic potential of test samples. Our results unveiled that at 100 μg/ml concentration of A1, A, B and C, there was no apparent change (statistically significant) in either the number of colonies or the size of colonies in comparison to vehicle-treated MCF-7 cells. These results indicate that A1, A, B, and C have little to no effect on the proliferative capacity of MCF-7 cells at 100 μg/ml concentration. Contrastingly, cells treated with the positive control camptothecin (0.5 μM) showed a drastic abrogation of proliferation and clonogenic potential as is evident from the huge reduction in the number of colonies. These studies showed cell survival assay properties of the sample A and A1.

A straightforward, large-scale, low-cost synthesis approach is used to fabricate novel hybrid nano-powders (ludox@CDs). The modified procedure was followed for the conversion of cationic surfactant (cetylpyridinium chloride) into highly fluorescent and amphiphilic CDs. These synthesized CDs accumulates a long-range emission band at 545 nm, QY∼34% and positive surface charge density (+63 mV). Afterward, to avoid aggregation and self-quenching effect in CDs during the hot-air drying process ludox HS-30 matrix were used to obtain fluorescent nano-powder. Then, a formulation is proposed to utilize ludox@CDs nano-powders as dusting reagents for the development of LFPs on glass surfaces under room conditions. The experimental studies stated that the fingermarks produced were clear and had no background staining when ludox@CDs nano-powder were used. In addition, distinct ridge elements of a fingermark as well as minutiae details were perceived that aid in fingermark recognition. In addition, the cytotoxic potential of these ludox@CDs nano-powders was examined on fR-2 breast cell lines to use them on a broad scale. The results showed that even at the highest dose, it demonstrated cell survival ranging from 18 to 33%. This suggested that ludox@CDs nano-powders were non-toxic and biocompatible in nature. On the other hand, ludox@CDs revealed a higher tendency to kill MCF-7 cancer cell lines than normal cells. These findings pave the way for ludox@CDs to be widely used in clinical studies for medicinal and forensic scientific advancements.

Shagun Kainth: Methodology, data interpretation Writing – original draft. Vishal Sharma: Fingermark detection. Madhulika Bhagat: Cytotoxicity studies, Investigation. and Soumen Basu: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Shagun is thankful to TIET-Virginia Tech Center of Excellence in Emerging Materials for fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2021.100659.
